mini_headings_covered: The strong and the weak nuclear forces
Use of nuclear fusion as source of energy in the sun

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The graph of binding energy per nucleon versus mass number peaks near iron-56 (Fe-56). What does this peak imply about energy release in nuclear reactions?

options.A: Fusion of elements lighter than iron releases energy; fission of elements heavier than iron releases energy.
options.B: Fusion of any elements releases energy; fission of any elements also releases energy.
options.C: Only fission reactions can release energy; fusion requires energy input.
options.D: Energy is released only when iron is formed; all other reactions absorb energy.

questions.hint: Compare the binding energy per nucleon of reactants and products.
questions.explanation: Lighter nuclei (below iron) can fuse to increase binding energy per nucleon, releasing energy. Heavier nuclei (above iron) can fission to increase binding energy per nucleon, releasing energy. Iron is the most stable.

source_trace.content_anchor: As nuclei get heavier than helium, their net binding energy per nucleon grows... reaches its peak at iron

explanations_tiered.definition: The peak in binding energy per nucleon at iron means that fusion of lighter elements and fission of heavier elements both release energy.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Nuclei move toward higher binding energy per nucleon. Lighter nuclei fuse to increase BE/nucleon; heavier nuclei fission to increase BE/nucleon. Both processes release energy.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Iron is the most stable nucleus. Lighter elements can combine (fuse) to become more stable and release energy. Heavier elements can split (fission) to become more stable and release energy.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The binding energy per nucleon curve increases from hydrogen to iron (fusion energy source) and decreases from iron to uranium (fission energy source). Iron is the most stable nucleus, with maximum BE/nucleon.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Nuclear binding energy per nucleon \(B/A\) is described by the semi-empirical mass formula (Bethe-Weizs\u00e4cker). The maximum at \(A=56\) (Fe) results from competition between volume, surface, Coulomb, and asymmetry terms.
reasoning_steps: Identify that higher BE/nucleon means greater stability.
Recognize that nuclei move toward higher stability.
Conclude that lighter nuclei fuse, heavier nuclei fission to increase BE/nucleon.
misconceptions: Thinking that all fusion or all fission releases energy (not true crossing iron).
Believing iron is the heaviest nucleus (it is not).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Fusion for A < 56, fission for A > 56 releases energy. Fe-56 most stable.

glossary_entry.definition: Maximum in binding energy per nucleon at A=56, indicating greatest stability.

flashcard.front: What does the BE/nucleon peak at iron-56 imply about energy release?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Fusion of lighter elements and fission of heavier elements release energy.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Energy release relative to iron peak?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Lighter fuse, heavier fission release energy.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Fe-56: most stable. Fusion below, fission above release energy.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use the BE/nucleon graph to illustrate.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the relationships between nuclear mass and binding energy
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Lighter nuclei fuse to increase BE/nucleon; heavier nuclei fission to increase BE/nucleon.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A person claims that alpha radiation is harmless because it cannot penetrate the skin. Why is this belief dangerous?

options.A: Alpha radiation can still cause burns on the skin surface.
options.B: If an alpha emitter is inhaled or ingested, it can cause severe internal damage due to high ionization.
options.C: Alpha radiation is actually the most penetrating type, contrary to common belief.
options.D: Alpha emitters always emit gamma radiation as well, which penetrates the skin.

questions.hint: Consider what happens if the source gets inside the body.
questions.explanation: While external alpha is blocked by skin, internal alpha emitters (e.g., radon gas, plutonium) deposit all their energy in a small volume of living tissue, causing severe cellular damage and cancer risk. Option A is incorrect (alpha doesn't cause skin burns externally).

source_trace.content_anchor: Alpha: Highly ionizing \u2013 very dangerous radiation poisoning and cancer possible. Outside body: absorbed by surface layer of dead skin cells \u2013 no danger

explanations_tiered.definition: Alpha emitters are dangerous if inhaled or ingested because they deposit high energy in a small volume of sensitive tissue.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: External alpha is safe due to dead skin layer. Internal alpha (e.g., radon, plutonium) has high linear energy transfer (LET), causing severe DNA damage and cancer.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you breathe in an alpha emitter, it gets stuck inside your lungs. Then its heavy, ionizing particles damage lung cells from the inside, which is very dangerous.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The range of 5 MeV alpha in tissue is ~50 \u03bcm, shorter than the thickness of the lung epithelium. All energy deposits in a single layer of cells, causing high local dose and high probability of cell transformation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The relative biological effectiveness (RBE) for alpha is 20, meaning 1 Gy of alpha causes the same biological damage as 20 Gy of gamma. Inhaled radon-222 and its progeny are the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking.
reasoning_steps: Recall that external alpha is blocked by skin.
Consider internal exposure routes (inhalation, ingestion).
Recognize that internal alpha deposits all energy in sensitive tissue.
Conclude that internal alpha is very dangerous.
misconceptions: Believing alpha is always harmless (ignores internal exposure).
Thinking alpha cannot cause cancer (it's a potent carcinogen if inhaled).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Alpha: external low risk, internal high risk (inhalation/ingestion).

glossary_entry.definition: Exposure from radioactive material inside the body, especially dangerous for alpha emitters.

flashcard.front: Why is a belief that alpha is harmless dangerous?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Inhaled or ingested alpha emitters cause severe internal damage.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is alpha radiation dangerous despite being stopped by skin?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Inhaled/ingested alpha emitters cause severe internal damage.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Exposure from inside body, very hazardous for alpha.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Alpha: internal high risk, external low risk.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss radon gas in homes as a real-world example.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Recognize and discuss dangers of ionizing radiation
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Internal alpha emitters cause severe damage. External alpha is safe due to skin.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: The problems posed by nuclear waste
questions.structural_anchor: The problems posed by nuclear waste
questions.question: Spent nuclear fuel contains plutonium-239 with a half-life of 24,100 years. Why does this long half-life pose a challenge for nuclear waste disposal?

options.A: The waste remains dangerously radioactive for tens of thousands of years.
options.B: Plutonium-239 decays into even more dangerous elements.
options.C: The long half-life means the waste emits extremely intense radiation.
options.D: Plutonium-239 cannot be shielded with any known material.

questions.hint: Long half-life means the material remains radioactive for a very long time.
questions.explanation: A long half-life (24,100 years for Pu-239) means the waste will remain hazardous for many thousands of years, requiring secure storage over geological timescales. Option B is not always true, C is false (long half-life implies lower specific activity), D is false.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: The problems posed by nuclear waste
source_trace.structural_element: The problems posed by nuclear waste
source_trace.content_anchor: Plutonium-239 has a half-life of 24100 years

explanations_tiered.definition: The long half-life of Pu-239 means nuclear waste remains dangerously radioactive for tens of thousands of years, requiring long-term storage solutions.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Half-life determines how long a radioactive material remains hazardous. Pu-239 requires isolation for about 10 half-lives (~240,000 years) to decay to safe levels.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a trash that stays dangerous for 24,000 years. We need to store it safely for longer than human civilization has existed.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Activity decreases exponentially. After 1 half-life (24,100 years), activity halves but is still significant. After 10 half-lives, activity reduces to ~0.1% of original, still requiring isolation from the biosphere.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The specific activity of Pu-239 is \(A = \lambda N_A/m_M = (\ln 2 / t_{1/2}) N_A / 239 = 2.3\times10^{-3}\) Ci/g. While not extremely intense, the long-term radiotoxicity requires deep geological disposal (e.g., Yucca Mountain, Onkalo).
reasoning_steps: Recall that half-life is time for activity to halve.
Long half-life means material remains radioactive for a long time.
Conclude that waste must be isolated for extended periods.
misconceptions: Some think long half-life = high activity (actually inversely related).
Believing that long half-life materials are easier to store (they require longer isolation).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Long t\u00bd \u2192 long-term hazard. Pu-239: 24,100 years.

glossary_entry.definition: Long-term storage of nuclear waste deep underground to isolate it from the biosphere.

flashcard.front: Why is Pu-239's 24,100-year half-life a disposal challenge?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Waste remains dangerously radioactive for tens of thousands of years.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Waste remains hazardous for tens of thousands of years.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Time for activity to halve; long half-life = long-term hazard.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare to Cs-137 (30 yr) and I-131 (8 d).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Investigate the benefits and hazards of nuclear energy production
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Long half-life means waste stays radioactive for thousands of years, requiring long-term storage.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 7.5 Safety Rules Against Hazards of Nuclear Radiation
questions.question: The IAEA defines nuclear safety as preventing accidents and mitigating consequences. Which of the following is a fundamental principle of radiation protection?

options.A: Maximize time near sources, minimize distance, avoid shielding
options.B: Minimize time, maximize distance, use appropriate shielding
options.C: Use only alpha-emitting sources, avoid gamma sources
options.D: Store all radioactive materials in open containers

questions.explanation: The three fundamental principles are: minimize exposure time, maximize distance from the source, and use appropriate shielding. Options A and D are opposite of safe practices, C is false (alpha is dangerous internally).

source_trace.section_anchor: 7.5 Safety Rules Against Hazards of Nuclear Radiation
source_trace.content_anchor: understand the radiation protection principles of time, distance and shielding

explanations_tiered.definition: Radiation protection principles: minimize time, maximize distance, use shielding.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Dose = dose rate \u00d7 time. Dose rate \u221d 1/r\u00b2. Shielding attenuates radiation. These three factors reduce exposure.
explanations_tiered.beginner: To stay safe around radiation: don't stay long, stay far away, and put something thick in between.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Time: halving time halves dose. Distance: doubling distance quarters dose (inverse-square). Shielding: lead for gamma, plastic/beta, paper/alpha.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The ALARA principle (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) incorporates time, distance, and shielding with economic and social factors. Regulatory dose limits are set based on these principles.
reasoning_steps: Recall the three standard principles.
Apply them to the options.
misconceptions: Confusing principles (e.g., maximizing time would increase dose).
Thinking distance doesn't matter much.

glossary_entry.definition: As Low As Reasonably Achievable \u2014 the guiding principle of radiation protection.

flashcard.front: What are the three principles of radiation protection?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Minimize time, maximize distance, use shielding.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Minimize time, maximize distance, use shielding.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce with examples: dental X-ray (lead apron, distance).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Implement safety rules against hazards of nuclear radiation
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Time (min), distance (max), shielding (use). Others violate safety principles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sample initially contains 1.0 \u00d7 10\u00b9\u00b9 atoms of a radioactive isotope with a half-life of 5 days. After 15 days, how many atoms remain?
questions.hint: 15 days = 3 half-lives. Each half-life halves the number.
questions.explanation: After 1 half-life: 5.0\u00d710\u00b9\u2070; after 2: 2.5\u00d710\u00b9\u2070; after 3: 1.25\u00d710\u00b9\u2070. Option A is initial, B is after 2 half-lives, D is after 1 half-life.

explanations_tiered.definition: After 3 half-lives, 1/8 of the original atoms remain.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: 15 days / 5 days per half-life = 3 half-lives. Fraction remaining = (1/2)\u00b3 = 1/8. N = 1.0\u00d710\u00b9\u00b9 / 8 = 1.25\u00d710\u00b9\u2070.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Every 5 days, the number halves. Start: 100 billion. After 5 days: 50 billion. After 10 days: 25 billion. After 15 days: 12.5 billion = 1.25\u00d710\u00b9\u2070.
reasoning_steps: Divide time by half-life to get number of half-lives.
Raise 1/2 to that power.
Multiply by initial number.
misconceptions: Subtracting half-lives instead of multiplying.
Forgetting to divide initial number.

flashcard.front: 1\u00d710\u00b9\u00b9 atoms, t\u00bd=5 d. After 15 d, how many remain?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In beta-plus (\u03b2\u207a) decay, a proton is converted into a neutron. What particle is emitted from the nucleus?
questions.hint: \u03b2\u207a decay emits a positively charged electron.
questions.explanation: Beta-plus decay emits a positron (the antimatter counterpart of the electron): \(p \rightarrow n + e^+ + \nu_e\). Option A is beta-minus, C is gamma, D is alpha.

source_trace.content_anchor: There are two beta decay types; beta minus (-) and beta plus (+). Beta plus is positron emission.

explanations_tiered.definition: Beta-plus decay emits a positron, the antimatter partner of the electron.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In \u03b2\u207a decay, a proton converts to a neutron, emitting a positron (e\u207a) and a neutrino. The atomic number decreases by 1.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A proton turns into a neutron and spits out a positively charged electron called a positron.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Positron emission occurs in proton-rich nuclei: \(p \rightarrow n + e^+ + \nu_e\). The positron is the antiparticle of the electron, with the same mass but opposite charge.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Positron emission is used in PET (Positron Emission Tomography) imaging. The positron annihilates with an electron, producing two 511 keV gamma rays emitted at 180\u00b0, allowing localization of the source.
reasoning_steps: Recall that \u03b2\u207a stands for beta-plus.
Remember that beta-plus emits a positron.
Select positron.
misconceptions: Confusing \u03b2\u207a with \u03b2\u207b (electron vs positron).
Thinking positrons are regular electrons with different mass.

glossary_entry.definition: Antimatter electron emitted in beta-plus decay.

flashcard.front: What particle is emitted in beta-plus decay?

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Distinguish between the three types of emissions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Positron (e\u207a). Option A is beta-minus, C is gamma, D is alpha.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 7.5 Safety Rules Against Hazards of Nuclear Radiation
questions.question: The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) defines nuclear security as the prevention of theft, sabotage, and unauthorized access. Why is this especially important for spent nuclear fuel?

options.A: Spent fuel contains plutonium that could be used in nuclear weapons.
options.B: Spent fuel is highly explosive and can detonate accidentally.
options.C: Spent fuel emits only alpha radiation, which is hard to detect.
options.D: Spent fuel has a very short half-life and decays rapidly.

questions.hint: Consider what fissile materials are present in spent fuel.
questions.explanation: Spent nuclear fuel contains plutonium-239 (fissile, weapon-usable) and other transuranic elements. Safeguards prevent diversion for weapons. Option B is false (not explosive), C false (emits gamma/beta), D false (long half-life).

source_trace.section_anchor: 7.5 Safety Rules Against Hazards of Nuclear Radiation
source_trace.content_anchor: prevention and detection of and response to, theft, sabotage, unauthorized access, illegal transfer or other malicious acts involving nuclear materials

explanations_tiered.definition: Spent fuel contains weapon-usable plutonium-239, requiring security to prevent proliferation.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Reprocessing spent fuel can separate plutonium. The IAEA safeguards aim to prevent diversion of nuclear materials from peaceful to military use.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Spent fuel leftovers contain plutonium that can be used to make nuclear bombs. Security is needed to stop bad actors from stealing it.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Plutonium-239 produced in reactors from U-238 neutron capture has a half-life of 24,100 years and is fissile. It must be protected under the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The IAEA safeguards system uses material accountancy, containment/surveillance, and inspections to detect diversion of significant quantities of nuclear material. The significant quantity for Pu-239 is 8 kg.
reasoning_steps: Recall that spent fuel contains Pu-239.
Recognize that Pu-239 is fissile and weapon-usable.
Conclude that security prevents theft for weapons.
misconceptions: Thinking spent fuel is no longer dangerous for weapons (it contains significant Pu).
Confusing nuclear waste with radiological dispersal devices ('dirty bombs').
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Spent fuel contains Pu-239 (weapon-usable) \u2192 security essential.

glossary_entry.definition: IAEA measures to prevent diversion of nuclear material from peaceful to military use.

flashcard.front: Why is security for spent nuclear fuel important?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Spent fuel contains plutonium-239 that could be used in nuclear weapons.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss the Non-Proliferation Treaty and IAEA role.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Implement safety rules against hazards of nuclear radiation
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Pu-239 in spent fuel is weapon-usable. Spent fuel is not explosive (B), emits gamma (C), long half-life (D).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: 7.1.1 Historical origins of the nucleus and its constituting particles
questions.question: If Rutherford had used an alpha particle source with higher kinetic energy, what effect would that have on the gold foil experiment results?

options.A: More alpha particles would be deflected at large angles because they penetrate closer to the nucleus.
options.B: No alpha particles would be deflected because higher energy particles ignore the nucleus.
options.C: The same proportion of alpha particles would be deflected at large angles, because deflection depends only on charge.
options.D: Fewer alpha particles would be deflected at large angles because higher energy reduces the effect of Coulomb repulsion.

questions.hint: Consider the Coulomb repulsion equation: higher speed means less time to be deflected.
questions.explanation: Higher kinetic energy means alpha particles pass the nucleus more quickly, experiencing less angular deflection from Coulomb repulsion. So large-angle deflections become less likely. Option A is opposite.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 7.1.1 Historical origins of the nucleus and its constituting particles
source_trace.content_anchor: Rutherford repeated the experiment many times

explanations_tiered.definition: Higher energy alpha particles are deflected less because they spend less time interacting with the nucleus.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rutherford scattering cross-section \(d\sigma/d\Omega \propto 1/E^2\). Higher energy reduces the probability of large-angle scattering.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Fast particles zip past the nucleus so quickly that they don't get pushed aside much, like a fast car barely turning the steering wheel.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The scattering angle \(\theta\) depends on impact parameter \(b\): \(\cot(\theta/2) = (4\pi\epsilon_0 E_k b)/(Z_1 Z_2 e^2)\). For fixed b, higher Ek gives smaller \u03b8. Large \u03b8 requires very small b, making it less probable at higher energy.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The differential cross-section for Rutherford scattering is \(d\sigma/d\Omega = (Z_1 Z_2 e^2/(16\pi\epsilon_0 E_k \sin^2(\theta/2)))^2\). At fixed \u03b8, \(d\sigma/d\Omega \propto 1/E_k^2\). Higher energy dramatically reduces large-angle scattering probability.
reasoning_steps: Recall Coulomb repulsion between alpha and nucleus.
Higher energy means faster speed.
Less time near nucleus means less deflection.
Conclude fewer large-angle deflections.
misconceptions: Thinking higher energy means more energy to 'overcome' repulsion, causing more deflection.
Confusing energy with cross-section relationship.

glossary_entry.definition: Probability of alpha particle deflection; inversely proportional to energy squared.

flashcard.front: Effect of higher alpha energy on gold foil scattering?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Higher alpha energy in gold foil experiment effect?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Deflection of alpha particles by nucleus; cross-section \u221d 1/E\u00b2.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Derive the 1/E\u00b2 dependence from Coulomb scattering formula.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss the historical origins of the nucleus
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Higher energy reduces scattering cross-section (1/E\u00b2), so fewer large-angle deflections.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a nuclear reactor, the chain reaction is controlled by inserting or withdrawing control rods made of neutron-absorbing materials such as boron or cadmium. What is the effect of fully inserting the control rods?

options.A: The reactor goes supercritical and power increases exponentially.
options.B: The reactor reaches criticality and operates at steady power.
options.C: The reactor becomes subcritical and the chain reaction stops.
options.D: The reactor converts from fission to fusion operation.

questions.hint: Control rods absorb neutrons; fully inserting them removes neutrons from the chain reaction.
questions.explanation: Fully inserting control rods maximizes neutron absorption, reducing the neutron multiplication factor k below 1 (subcritical), stopping the chain reaction. Option A is supercritical (withdrawn rods), B is critical (partial insertion), D is false.

source_trace.content_anchor: control the rate of reaction, or to halt it

explanations_tiered.definition: Fully inserting control rods makes the reactor subcritical (k < 1), stopping the chain reaction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Control rods absorb neutrons. When fully inserted, neutron absorption exceeds production, causing the reaction to shut down.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Control rods are like brakes. Pushing them all the way in stops the chain reaction completely.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The neutron multiplication factor k: fully inserted rods \u2192 k < 1 (subcritical) \u2192 reaction stops. Partially inserted rods \u2192 k = 1 (critical) \u2192 steady power. Withdrawn rods \u2192 k > 1 (supercritical) \u2192 power increase.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Reactivity \u03c1 = (k-1)/k. Control rods introduce negative reactivity. Full insertion gives \u03c1 < 0, shutting down the reactor. Scram (emergency shutdown) uses gravity or springs to insert rods rapidly.
reasoning_steps: Recall that control rods absorb neutrons.
More absorber = fewer neutrons for fission.
Fully inserted = maximal absorption = chain reaction stops.

formula_analysis.formula: k = neutrons in generation n+1 / neutrons in generation n
formula_analysis.name: Neutron multiplication factor

misconceptions: Thinking control rods slow neutrons (that's the moderator).
Believing fully inserted rods increase power.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Fully inserted control rods \u2192 subcritical \u2192 chain reaction stops.

glossary_entry.definition: Emergency shutdown of a nuclear reactor by fully inserting control rods.

flashcard.front: Effect of fully inserting control rods in a reactor?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Reactor becomes subcritical; chain reaction stops.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain the terms subcritical, critical, supercritical.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss the use of artificial nuclear reaction
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Fully inserted rods make reactor subcritical (k<1), stopping chain reaction. A is supercritical (rods out), B is critical (partial insertion), D false.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A patient receives an intravenous injection of a radioactive tracer for a PET scan. The tracer emits positrons that annihilate with electrons, producing gamma rays detected externally. Which isotope is commonly used for this purpose?
questions.hint: PET tracers are short-lived positron emitters.
questions.explanation: Fluorine-18 is a common PET tracer (half-life 110 min). It emits positrons that annihilate to produce 511 keV gamma rays. Cobalt-60 is a gamma emitter for teletherapy, I-131 for thyroid, U-235 is fission fuel.

source_trace.content_anchor: positron emitting nucleotides (PET) are used to scan

explanations_tiered.definition: Fluorine-18 (F-18) is a positron-emitting isotope used in PET scans, with a 110-minute half-life.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: F-18 decays by \u03b2\u207a emission to oxygen-18. The positron annihilates with an electron, producing two 511 keV gamma rays detected by the PET scanner.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The PET scanner needs a tracer that spits out positrons. Fluorine-18 is a common choice because it works well with the chemistry of the body and doesn't last too long.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: F-18 is produced in a cyclotron via \(^{18}O(p,n)^{18}F\). It is commonly attached to fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) to image glucose metabolism in cancer cells.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Coincidence detection of the two 511 keV gamma rays (emitted at 180\u00b0) allows reconstruction of the annihilation point with high spatial resolution (~4-6 mm). The short half-life requires on-site cyclotron or generator.
reasoning_steps: Recall that PET uses positron emitters.
Identify common PET isotopes (F-18, C-11, O-15, N-13).
Select F-18 from the options.
misconceptions: Confusing PET with SPECT (which uses single gamma emitters like Tc-99m).
Thinking any radioisotope can be used for PET (requires positron emission).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: PET: positron emitter (e.g., F-18) \u2192 annihilation \u2192 511 keV gamma pairs.

glossary_entry.definition: Imaging technique using positron-emitting tracers and coincidence detection of annihilation gamma rays.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which isotope is commonly used for PET scans?
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain application of radioactivity to society
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. F-18 is a common PET tracer (positron emitter). Co-60 is teletherapy, I-131 thyroid, U-235 fuel.
